title.en: Spec-First Vibe Coding: Decide What 'Done' Means Before You Prompt

description.en: The biggest leverage in AI coding isn't a clever prompt — it's writing the spec first: exact inputs, outputs, edge cases, and ideally the tests, BEFORE you ask for an implementation. A precise spec turns 'make it work' into a verifiable target you can check in one second.

story_variants.en:
## The Two Prompts

Watch two developers ask an AI for the *same* function. The first types:

> "Write a Python function to make a URL slug from a title."

The second types:

> "Write `slugify(text)`. It lowercases the text, replaces runs of spaces with a single hyphen, strips out every character that isn't a letter, digit, or hyphen, and trims leading/trailing hyphens. `slugify('Hello, World!')` must return `'hello-world'`. `slugify('Python 3.12 Rocks!')` must return `'python-312-rocks'`. Make this assertion pass: `assert slugify('  A--B  ') == 'a-b'`."

Both get working-looking code back. But only the second developer **knows what they got.** The first will spend the next ten minutes squinting at output, discovering that *their* AI decided to keep the comma, or used underscores, or left the title in uppercase. They never said. So the AI guessed — and a guess that *looks* right is the most expensive thing in software.

This is the core move of spec-first vibe coding: **decide what 'done' means before you prompt.**

## A Spec Is a Verifiable Target

A vague prompt asks the AI to *both* invent the requirements *and* implement them. You've handed away the one decision only you can make — what the thing is actually supposed to do — to a pattern-completion engine that will cheerfully pick the most statistically average interpretation.

A spec separates the two jobs. **You** define the target. **The AI** hits it. And because the target is concrete, you can check the result instantly instead of arguing with code in your head.

A good spec answers four questions out loud:

| Question | Vague prompt | Spec-first prompt |
|----------|--------------|-------------------|
| **Inputs?** | "a title" | `text: str`, may be empty, may have punctuation/extra spaces |
| **Outputs?** | "a slug" | lowercase, hyphen-separated, `[a-z0-9-]` only |
| **Examples?** | none | `'Hello, World!' -> 'hello-world'` |
| **Edge cases?** | none | empty string, leading/trailing spaces, runs of spaces, symbols |

Notice the spec is shorter to *read* than the bugs it prevents are to *debug*.

## Write the Assertions First

The strongest version of spec-first borrows the oldest trick in engineering: **write the test before the code.** You don't describe the behavior in prose and hope — you pin it down as runnable assertions:

```python
assert slugify('Hello, World!') == 'hello-world'
assert slugify('Python 3.12 Rocks!') == 'python-312-rocks'
assert slugify('  Multiple   Spaces  ') == 'multiple-spaces'
assert slugify('') == ''
```

Now your prompt becomes almost trivial: *"Make these assertions pass."* Three things happen at once:

1. **The target is unambiguous.** There is no interpretation left for the AI to get wrong. The examples *are* the requirement.
2. **Verification is free.** The moment the AI returns code, you run the asserts. Green means done. Red means not done. No squinting.
3. **You caught your own confusion early.** Writing `assert slugify('A & B') == ?` forces *you* to decide what the ampersand becomes — *before* a single line of implementation exists. Half of all bugs are really just a question nobody asked at spec time.

## The Loop

Spec-first vibe coding is a tight, fast loop:

- **Spec** — write the inputs, outputs, examples, and edge cases.
- **Assert** — turn the examples into runnable assertions (the definition of *done*).
- **Prompt** — "implement this so these tests pass."
- **Run** — execute the asserts. Green? Ship. Red? Paste the failure back and let the AI fix it against a target it can now see too.

The magic is that the AI is no longer guessing your intent — it's chasing a finish line *you both* can see. When a test fails, you don't debate; you point at the red.

> **Key idea:** The leverage in AI coding is upstream of the prompt. Decide what 'done' means — as concrete inputs, outputs, and assertions — *before* you ask for code. A spec turns a vague 'make it work' into a target the AI can hit and you can verify in one second. Write the test first, then prompt 'make it pass.'

left.label: Vague: 'Write a function to make a URL slug.' -> AI guesses; you squint at output

right.label: Spec-first: exact inputs, outputs, examples + 'make these asserts pass' -> verify in 1 second

description.en: Same function, same model. The spec-first developer KNOWS what they got; the vague one has to discover it.

nodes.label: The Spec-First Loop
children: 4. Run: green = done, red = point at it

animation_sequence.code:
def slugify(text):
    import re
    t = re.sub(r'[^a-z0-9\s-]', '', text.strip().lower())
    return re.sub(r'[\s-]+', '-', t).strip('-')

assert slugify('Hello, World!') == 'hello-world'
assert slugify('Python 3.12 Rocks!') == 'python-312-rocks'
print('done')

executionSteps.description: The assertion IS the spec. Green means the target was hit.
Digits kept, symbols stripped — exactly as specified up front.
No squinting required. The tests already proved it's done.

output: A vague prompt makes the AI invent BOTH the requirements and the code.
A spec separates them: you define 'done', the AI just hits the target.
Write the assertions first, then prompt: 'make these pass'.
Green = done. Red = point at it. No arguing with code in your head.

instruction.en: Spec-first in action. You're given a precise spec and a stub. Implement slugify(text) so it satisfies the spec: (1) lowercase the text; (2) strip leading/trailing whitespace; (3) remove every character that isn't a letter, digit, space, or hyphen; (4) collapse any run of spaces/hyphens into a single hyphen; (5) trim leading/trailing hyphens. The assertions in the test panel ARE the definition of done — make them all pass. With the given data, slugify('  Hello, World!  ') returns 'hello-world'. Expected output: hello-world

practice_challenges.starter_code:
import re

# SPEC:
#   slugify(text) -> str
#   - lowercase, strip outer whitespace
#   - remove any char that is NOT a letter, digit, space, or hyphen
#   - collapse runs of spaces/hyphens into a single '-'
#   - trim leading/trailing '-'
#   Examples:
#     'Hello, World!'        -> 'hello-world'
#     'Python 3.12 Rocks!'   -> 'python-312-rocks'
#     '  A--B  '             -> 'a-b'
def slugify(text):
    pass  # implement to satisfy the spec above

print(slugify("  Hello, World!  "))

en: Start by normalizing: text.strip().lower() handles outer whitespace and casing in one step.
Remove unwanted characters with a regex: re.sub(r'[^a-z0-9\s-]', '', text) deletes anything that isn't a lowercase letter, digit, whitespace, or hyphen.
Collapse runs of spaces AND hyphens into one hyphen: re.sub(r'[\s-]+', '-', text). This turns 'A--B' and 'A  B' both into 'A-B'.
Finally trim stray hyphens from the ends with .strip('-') so inputs like '-Hi-' don't keep a leading/trailing dash.

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert slugify('Hello, World!') == 'hello-world'
assert slugify('Python 3.12 Rocks!') == 'python-312-rocks'
assert slugify('  A--B  ') == 'a-b'
assert slugify('') == ''

q.en: What is the core move of spec-first vibe coding?

en: Writing the most detailed prompt you can after seeing the code
Deciding what 'done' means — exact inputs, outputs, and examples — BEFORE you prompt
Letting the AI choose the requirements so you write less
Reading the generated code very carefully three times

q.en: Why is a vague prompt like 'make a URL slug' risky?

en: The AI refuses to answer prompts that are too short
It forces the AI to invent BOTH the requirements and the implementation, so it guesses your intent
It always produces code that won't run
Slugs are impossible to generate in Python

q.en: What is the advantage of writing the assertions BEFORE the implementation?

en: It makes the function run faster at runtime
The examples become an unambiguous target, verification is free, and you catch your own confusion early
It lets you skip running the code entirely
It replaces the need for the function itself

q.en: In the spec-first loop, what do you do when a test comes back red?

en: Argue with the code in your head until it seems right
Delete the failing assertion so everything passes
Paste the failure back and let the AI fix it against a target you both can now see
Rewrite the whole spec from scratch
